unmatchedSentences.text: For nearly 30 years, Ireland has teased a desperately needed metro to tackle its congestion nightmare. But, without really making any proper inroads towards building it. And with Dublin's travel problem getting worse and worse, what the hell's being done to fix this city? >> >> It's not like Ireland doesn't know that Dublin needs help with its travel horror show.

It's actually one of the last European capitals without a direct train to its airport. You've got to take a taxi, get a lift, or jump on the bus, which seems a bit ridiculous for a city of this scale.
Jeez, man. This is this is this is a joke. So, I'm sensing a a wee bit of discontent.

>> >> As the money dried up due to the global recession, Metro North slipped further and further down the priority list. Various iterations have since followed. In 2018, an updated plan offered hope that Dubliners could get around the city via an underground train line by 2027.

And while they were right, because that would be next year, and no groundwork had started yet, why set unrealistic deadlines to begin with? Construction should then have gotten underway by 2025, but the latest metro designs didn't gain planning permission until October last year.

>> >> Given this is the country's first ever metro, authorities are obviously keen to get it right. Spending billions on a project like this and then getting the route wrong would be disastrous, especially when it's already taken quite a lot of time and has already been a bit of a debacle.

When it was announced the last time, there was a a researcher in zoology that basically said, "The time that we've been talking about the metro and to construct a metro, a snail could have gone from Dublin city center, I passed the airport, and do the whole line based upon how long it's taken us to get to where we are right now." So, I think right now, the money is there, the ambition is there, the support is there.

Roads have to be closed or converted to one way for weeks and even months on end. One of the appeals of Dublin is those picturesque narrow streets. But in the 11th most congested city on the planet, more chaos than necessary is just asking for trouble.

>> >> Now, all of that work is necessary to build a much-needed piece of infrastructure. And for construction fans like myself, it's a pretty cool project to be able to watch, follow, and check in on. But, the process is incredibly disruptive. The tunneling, the rerouting of existing travel options, the noise, the dust, the list goes on.

These projects are critical pieces of infrastructure um for our country, and not being able to deliver them is crippling. Crippling our cities, crippling our infrastructure, crippling our ability to be able to deliver the housing and transport, and meet our climate goals.

longSentences.text: For nearly 30 years, Ireland has teased a desperately needed metro to tackle its congestion nightmare. But, without really making any proper inroads towards building it. And with Dublin's travel problem getting worse and worse, what the hell's being done to fix this city? >> >> It's not like Ireland doesn't know that Dublin needs help with its travel horror show.

>> >> As the money dried up due to the global recession, Metro North slipped further and further down the priority list. Various iterations have since followed. In 2018, an updated plan offered hope that Dubliners could get around the city via an underground train line by 2027.

And while they were right, because that would be next year, and no groundwork had started yet, why set unrealistic deadlines to begin with? Construction should then have gotten underway by 2025, but the latest metro designs didn't gain planning permission until October last year.

So, governments and the agencies that are supporting and and building building and delivering metro are proceeding as if this piece of infrastructure is going to happen. We're hiring people, the tender documents have been put together to bring consortia to the city to deliver our metro.

While I've been using RoundDesigns all-in-one CAD software to create my dream home right smack in the middle of Dublin, which by the way, guys, I only need like a trillion YouTube video views to pay for, this platform is actually the ideal workmate for any type of project, including, for example, a massive new metro line.

Whether you're an urban planner designing the next great metro line, or like me, you just want to draw up a swanky Irish penthouse, you can try Round Design for free using the link in the description or by scanning the QR code on screen now.

When it was announced the last time, there was a a researcher in zoology that basically said, "The time that we've been talking about the metro and to construct a metro, a snail could have gone from Dublin city center, I passed the airport, and do the whole line based upon how long it's taken us to get to where we are right now." So, I think right now, the money is there, the ambition is there, the support is there.

But, ask Torontonians who waited nearly 20 years for work to start on Eglinton Crosstown or the city of Thessaloniki which waited a century for its metro and you'll realize that these projects can take an absolute age to happen and then all of a sudden the city turns into a work site.

Roads have to be closed or converted to one way for weeks and even months on end. One of the appeals of Dublin is those picturesque narrow streets. But in the 11th most congested city on the planet, more chaos than necessary is just asking for trouble.

>> >> Now, all of that work is necessary to build a much-needed piece of infrastructure. And for construction fans like myself, it's a pretty cool project to be able to watch, follow, and check in on. But, the process is incredibly disruptive. The tunneling, the rerouting of existing travel options, the noise, the dust, the list goes on.

The section of track that runs underneath the area between Collins Avenue and Griffith Park is nearly 1,500 m. I.E., it gets a big shake of the head from the EU, and that's why an intervention shaft has to be created no more than a kilometer from either of these stations.

Now, this isn't some agenda against Ireland's approach to construction. If any dense city anywhere in the world were to start building open tunnels in the streets or constructing new stations in the middle of its urban center, there would, of course, be a space issue.

This project has got to coexist around everyday life in this city. But, given how most of these metro builds go around the world, and the fact that Ireland is going to be doing this for the first time, don't be surprised if the fair city is a work site for quite a while.

These projects are critical pieces of infrastructure um for our country, and not being able to deliver them is crippling. Crippling our cities, crippling our infrastructure, crippling our ability to be able to deliver the housing and transport, and meet our climate goals.
